title: Iran Formally Refuses IAEA Inspection of Bombed Nuclear Sites
detail: Mohammad Eslami, head of Iran's Atomic Energy Organization, states Iran will not comply with IAEA demands to inspect nuclear sites damaged in the June war. Eslami demands the Agency first clarify whether the US-Israeli military strikes on nuclear facilities were authorized under international law, and if not, formally condemn them and establish clear procedures for 'post-war inspections.' The refusal deepens the verification crisis, leaving the IAEA unable to determine the status of Iran's enriched uranium stockpile or reconstruction activities.

title: Eslami Accuses IAEA of Political Pressure Over Inspections
detail: Expanding on Iran's inspection refusal, Eslami accuses the IAEA of applying 'political and psychological pressure' by demanding access to bombed nuclear sites without first addressing the legality of the strikes. He argues that inspection protocols designed for intact facilities cannot be applied to sites damaged by bunker-buster munitions, and that new frameworks must be developed. Western diplomats dismiss the argument as a stalling tactic designed to shield reconstruction activities from scrutiny.

title: Diplomatic Stalemate as Iran Rejects Zero-Enrichment Demand
detail: Despite mediation efforts by Gulf states including Oman and Qatar, Iran refuses to resume nuclear negotiations so long as the US maintains its demand for zero enrichment. Tehran insists on its right to enrich uranium for peaceful purposes under the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty, while Washington and its allies argue that Iran's enrichment to 60% U-235, near weapons grade, demonstrates the program's military dimensions. The impasse leaves no diplomatic pathway to resolve the nuclear crisis as 2025 draws to a close.
